category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Model Paper 01/Part A - General Aptitude

info.text: Part A: General Aptitude (Q1–15). Marks: +2 correct, −0.5 incorrect. Attempt any 12.

questiontext.text: An article is bought for Rs. 480 and sold for Rs. 600. What is the profit percentage?

name.text: P01 Q2 - Time and work pipes

questiontext.text: Pipe A fills a tank in 12 hours and Pipe B empties it in 18 hours. If both pipes are open simultaneously, how long will it take to fill the tank?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 36 hours

Net rate = 1/12 − 1/18 = 3/36 − 2/36 = 1/36. Time = 36 hours.

questiontext.text: A principal of Rs. 4000 earns Rs. 1200 as simple interest in 3 years. What is the annual rate of interest?
Planet : Solar System :: Cell : ?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) Body

A planet is a component of a solar system; a cell is a component of a body (organism).

name.text: P01 Q5 - Age problem father son

questiontext.text: A father is 30 years older than his son. Five years ago, the father was four times the age of his son. What is the son's present age?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 15 years

Let son's age = x. Father's age = x+30. Five years ago: x+30−5 = 4(x−5) ⟹ x+25 = 4x−20 ⟹ 3x = 45 ⟹ x = 15.

name.text: P01 Q6 - Letter series A C F J

questiontext.text: Find the next letter in the series: A, C, F, J, O, ?
A car travels from city A to city B at 60 km/h and returns at 40 km/h. What is the average speed for the entire journey?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 48 km/h

Average speed = 2xy/(x+y) = 2×60×40/(60+40) = 4800/100 = 48 km/h.

questiontext.text: In how many ways can 6 people be seated around a circular table?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 120

Circular arrangements = (n−1)! = 5! = 120.

questiontext.text: A man points to a woman and says "Her mother is the only daughter of my mother." How is the woman related to the man?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) Niece

Only daughter of the man's mother = the man's sister. The woman's mother is the man's sister, so the woman is the man's niece.

questiontext.text: What is the area of a right triangle if: (I) the hypotenuse is 10 cm, (II) one leg is 6 cm?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) Both I and II together are sufficient

With hypotenuse 10 and one leg 6: other leg = √(100−36)=8. Area = (1/2)×6×8 = 24 cm².

answer.text: Either I or II alone is sufficient
Both I and II together are sufficient

questiontext.text: A mixture of 60 litres contains milk and water in the ratio 3:2. How much water must be added to make the ratio 3:4?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 24 litres

Milk = 36 L, Water = 24 L. After adding x L water: 36/(24+x)=3/4 ⟹ 144=72+3x ⟹ x=24.

questiontext.text: A man walks 10 km South, then 6 km East, then 10 km North. How far is he from the starting point?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 6 km

South 10, then North 10 cancels. Net displacement: 6 km East. Distance = 6 km.

questiontext.text: A card is drawn at random from a standard deck of 52 cards. What is the probability of drawing a face card (Jack, Queen, or King)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) 3/13

Face cards: 4 Jacks + 4 Queens + 4 Kings = 12. P = 12/52 = 3/13.

questiontext.text: What comes next: 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, ?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 36

Pattern: n² for n=1,2,3,4,5,6. Next: 6²=36.

name.text: P01 Q15 - Odd one out tool

questiontext.text: Which is the odd one out? Violin, Guitar, Flute, Sitar, Veena

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) Flute

Violin, Guitar, Sitar, and Veena are all string instruments. Flute is a wind instrument.

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Model Paper 01/Part B - Core Mathematics

info.text: Part B: Core Mathematics (Q16–40). Marks: +3 correct, −1 incorrect. Attempt any 20.

questiontext.text: The sequence \(a_n = \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{n}\right)^n\) converges to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) e

This is the definition of Euler's number: \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\left(1+\frac{1}{n}\right)^n = e \approx 2.718.\)

name.text: P01 Q17 - Cauchy sequence in Q

questiontext.text: Every Cauchy sequence in \(\mathbb{R}\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) converges in ℝ

ℝ is complete: every Cauchy sequence in ℝ converges to a real limit. This is the completeness axiom of ℝ.

answer.text: is bounded but may not converge

questiontext.text: Which of the following functions is uniformly continuous on \((0,1)\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) f(x) = x²

f(x)=x² is continuous on the closed bounded interval [0,1], hence uniformly continuous on (0,1). f(x)=1/x and f(x)=tan(πx/2) blow up near boundary points. f(x)=sin(1/x) oscillates rapidly near 0.

questiontext.text: A bounded function \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is Riemann integrable if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) the set of discontinuities of f has measure zero

By Lebesgue's theorem, a bounded function is Riemann integrable iff it is continuous almost everywhere (i.e., its discontinuity set has Lebesgue measure zero).

answer.text: the set of discontinuities of \(f\) has Lebesgue measure zero

questiontext.text: If \(f:[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous with \(f(0)=−3\) and \(f(1)=5\), by the Intermediate Value Theorem, there exists \(c\in(0,1)\) such that \(f(c)=\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 0

IVT guarantees f attains every value between f(0)=−3 and f(1)=5. Since −3 < 0 < 5, there exists c with f(c)=0. The value 6 is outside the range [−3,5]; −4 is also outside.

questiontext.text: If \(A\) is an \(n\times n\) matrix, then \(\det(kA) =\) ?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) kⁿ det(A)

Each row of kA is k times the corresponding row of A. Factoring k from each of the n rows: det(kA) = kⁿ det(A).

questiontext.text: If \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of a square matrix \(A\), then which of the following is also an eigenvalue of \(A\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) λ is an eigenvalue of Aᵀ

det(Aᵀ − λI) = det((A − λI)ᵀ) = det(A − λI). So A and Aᵀ have the same characteristic polynomial and hence the same eigenvalues.

questiontext.text: For a linear map \(T: \mathbb{R}^5 \to \mathbb{R}^3\), if \(\mathrm{rank}(T) = 2\), what is the nullity of \(T\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 3

Rank-Nullity Theorem: rank(T) + nullity(T) = dim(domain) = 5. So nullity = 5 − 2 = 3.

questiontext.text: A real symmetric matrix is always:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) diagonalizable with real eigenvalues

By the Spectral Theorem, every real symmetric matrix is orthogonally diagonalizable with all eigenvalues real.

name.text: P01 Q25 - Order of group element

questiontext.text: In the group \(\mathbb{Z}_{12}\) (integers mod 12 under addition), what is the order of the element 8?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 3

Order of 8 in ℤ₁₂ = 12/gcd(8,12) = 12/4 = 3. Check: 8+8+8=24≡0 (mod 12). ✓

questiontext.text: By Lagrange's theorem, if \(H\) is a subgroup of a finite group \(G\), then \(|H|\) divides \(|G|\). Which of the following is a possible order for a subgroup of a group of order 24?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 8

Divisors of 24 are: 1,2,3,4,6,8,12,24. So 8 divides 24. The number 7 does not divide 24.

questiontext.text: A subgroup \(N\) of group \(G\) is normal if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) gNg⁻¹ = N for all g∈G

This is the definition of a normal subgroup. It ensures the quotient G/N is a well-defined group.

questiontext.text: The Cauchy-Riemann equations for \(f(z)=u(x,y)+iv(x,y)\) are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) u_x = v_y and u_y = −v_x

The CR equations are necessary and sufficient (with continuity of partials) for f to be holomorphic.

questiontext.text: By Cauchy's integral formula, if \(f\) is holomorphic inside and on a simple closed contour \(C\) and \(a\) is inside \(C\), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) f(a) = (1/2πi)∮_C f(z)/(z−a) dz

This is Cauchy's integral formula, a cornerstone of complex analysis.

questiontext.text: The residue of \(f(z) = \dfrac{e^z}{z^2}\) at \(z=0\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) 1

Laurent expansion: e^z/z² = (1 + z + z²/2! + ...)/z² = 1/z² + 1/z + 1/2 + ... The coefficient of 1/z is 1, so Res = 1.

questiontext.text: In the standard topology on \(\mathbb{R}\), which of the following sets is open?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) (0,1)

(0,1) is an open interval = open set. [0,1] is closed (contains boundary), {0,1} is discrete (closed), ℚ is neither open nor closed in ℝ.

questiontext.text: Which of the following subsets of \(\mathbb{R}\) is compact?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) [0,1]

By Heine-Borel, compact subsets of ℝ are exactly the closed and bounded sets. [0,1] is closed and bounded. (0,1) is not closed. ℝ is unbounded. ℤ is closed but unbounded.

name.text: P01 Q33 - ODE linear second order

questiontext.text: The general solution of \(y'' - 5y' + 6y = 0\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) c₁e²ˣ + c₂e³ˣ

Characteristic equation: r² − 5r + 6 = 0 ⟹ (r−2)(r−3)=0 ⟹ r=2,3. General solution: c₁e²ˣ + c₂e³ˣ.

questiontext.text: The heat equation \(u_t = k\,u_{xx}\) on the real line with initial condition \(u(x,0)=f(x)\) has solution given by convolution with the heat kernel. The heat kernel \(K(x,t)\) for \(t>0\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) K(x,t) = (1/√(4πkt)) exp(−x²/(4kt))

This is the fundamental solution (Green's function) of the heat equation on ℝ.

questiontext.text: If \(X\sim N(\mu,\sigma^2)\), what is \(P(\mu-\sigma < X < \mu+\sigma)\) approximately?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 0.6827

For a normal distribution, approximately 68.27% of values lie within 1 standard deviation of the mean (the 68-95-99.7 rule).

questiontext.text: A disease affects 1% of a population. A test has 95% sensitivity (true positive rate) and 90% specificity (true negative rate). If a person tests positive, what is the probability they have the disease (approximately)?
Which of the following metric spaces is connected?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) ℝ with the standard metric

ℝ is connected (cannot be split into two disjoint nonempty open sets). ℚ is totally disconnected. Discrete metric on any set with ≥2 elements is disconnected. ℤ with standard metric is disconnected.

questiontext.text: The radius of convergence of the power series \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{x^n}{n!}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) ∞

By ratio test: |a_{n+1}/a_n| = |x|/(n+1) → 0 for all x. So the series converges for all x∈ℝ (this is eˣ). Radius = ∞.

questiontext.text: The series \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^p}\) converges if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) p > 1

The p-series converges for p > 1 and diverges for p ≤ 1 (by the integral test: ∫₁^∞ x^{−p}dx converges iff p > 1).

questiontext.text: The characteristic of the ring \(\mathbb{Z}_6\) (integers mod 6) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 6

The characteristic of ℤₙ is n. 6·1 = 6 ≡ 0 (mod 6), and no smaller positive integer k satisfies k·1 ≡ 0 (mod 6).

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Model Paper 01/Part C - Advanced Mathematics

info.text: Part C: Advanced Mathematics (Q41–55). MSQ: +4 full credit, +2 partial, −1 wrong. Attempt any 10.

name.text: P01 Q41 MSQ - Real analysis sequences

questiontext.text: Which of the following statements about sequences in \(\mathbb{R}\) are TRUE?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(4)

(1) TRUE: Every bounded sequence in ℝ has a convergent subsequence (Bolzano-Weierstrass). (2) TRUE: Every convergent sequence is Cauchy and bounded. (3) FALSE: A Cauchy sequence in ℚ need not converge in ℚ (e.g., approximations to √2). (4) TRUE: lim sup and lim inf always exist (in [-∞,+∞]) for any bounded sequence.

answer.text: Every bounded sequence in \(\mathbb{R}\) has a convergent subsequence.
Every convergent sequence in \(\mathbb{R}\) is bounded.
Every Cauchy sequence in \(\mathbb{Q}\) converges in \(\mathbb{Q}\).
For any bounded sequence \((a_n)\), both \(\limsup a_n\) and \(\liminf a_n\) exist as real numbers.

name.text: P01 Q42 MSQ - Linear algebra eigenvalues

questiontext.text: Let \(A\) be an \(n\times n\) real matrix. Which of the following are TRUE?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: tr(A) = sum of eigenvalues. (2) TRUE: det(A) = product of eigenvalues. (3) TRUE: If A is real symmetric, all eigenvalues are real. (4) TRUE: A and Aᵀ have the same characteristic polynomial hence same eigenvalues.

answer.text: The trace of \(A\) equals the sum of its eigenvalues.
The determinant of \(A\) equals the product of its eigenvalues.
If \(A\) is real symmetric, all its eigenvalues are real.

questiontext.text: Which of the following statements about groups are TRUE?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(4)

(1) TRUE: Every subgroup of index 2 is normal (since it has only 2 cosets). (2) TRUE: Every cyclic group is abelian. (3) FALSE: S₃ is non-abelian with order 6. (4) TRUE: A group of prime order p is cyclic and isomorphic to ℤₚ.

answer.text: Every subgroup of index 2 in a group \(G\) is normal.
Every group of order 6 is abelian.
Every group of prime order is cyclic.

name.text: P01 Q44 MSQ - Complex analysis holomorphic

questiontext.text: Let \(f\) be holomorphic on the open unit disk \(\mathbb{D}\). Which of the following are TRUE?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Holomorphic functions are infinitely differentiable (analytic). (2) TRUE: If f is holomorphic and |f|=constant on D, then f is constant (by open mapping theorem / CR equations). (3) TRUE: Liouville's theorem. (4) TRUE: Identity theorem: zeros of a non-zero holomorphic function are isolated.

answer.text: If \(|f|\) is constant on \(\mathbb{D}\), then \(f\) is constant.
A bounded entire function must be constant.
The zeros of a non-identically-zero holomorphic function on \(\mathbb{D}\) are isolated.

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about compact topological spaces?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3)

(1) TRUE: Continuous image of a compact set is compact. (2) TRUE: A closed subset of a compact space is compact. (3) TRUE: A compact subset of a Hausdorff space is closed. (4) FALSE: An open subset of a compact space need not be compact (e.g., (0,1) ⊂ [0,1]).

answer.text: The continuous image of a compact space is compact.
A closed subset of a compact space is compact.
A compact subset of a Hausdorff space is closed.
Every open subset of a compact space is compact.

questiontext.text: Consider the IVP \(y'=f(x,y),\; y(x_0)=y_0\). Which conditions guarantee existence and uniqueness of a solution?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (2),(4)

(1) FALSE: Continuity of f alone guarantees existence (Peano) but not uniqueness. (2) TRUE: Lipschitz condition in y is sufficient for uniqueness (Picard-Lindelöf). (3) FALSE: Boundedness alone is not sufficient. (4) TRUE: Continuous partial derivative ∂f/∂y implies Lipschitz locally, giving local existence and uniqueness.

answer.text: \(f\) is continuous in both \(x\) and \(y\) (Peano condition).
\(f\) satisfies a Lipschitz condition in \(y\) uniformly in \(x\).
\(f\) is bounded on a rectangle containing \((x_0,y_0)\).

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about Lebesgue measure on \(\mathbb{R}\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(4)

(1) TRUE: Any countable set has measure zero. (2) TRUE: Lebesgue measure is translation invariant. (3) FALSE: The Cantor set has measure zero but is uncountable (not countable). (4) TRUE: Every open set in ℝ is a countable union of disjoint open intervals.

answer.text: Every countable subset of \(\mathbb{R}\) has Lebesgue measure zero.
The Cantor set is countable (since it has measure zero).
Every open set in \(\mathbb{R}\) is a countable union of disjoint open intervals.

name.text: P01 Q48 MSQ - Rings and ideals

questiontext.text: Which of the following statements about rings are TRUE?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Every field is an integral domain (no zero divisors). (2) TRUE: Every PID is a UFD. (3) TRUE: In ℤ, every ideal is principal (generated by one element). (4) TRUE: The quotient R/I is a field iff I is a maximal ideal.

answer.text: Every field is an integral domain.
Every principal ideal domain (PID) is a unique factorization domain (UFD).
Every ideal in \(\mathbb{Z}\) is a principal ideal.
For a commutative ring \(R\) with unity and ideal \(I\), \(R/I\) is a field if and only if \(I\) is maximal.

questiontext.text: For a function \(f\in L^2[-\pi,\pi]\) with Fourier series \(f(x)\sim \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} c_n e^{inx}\), which are TRUE?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Parseval's identity: ∑|cₙ|² = (1/2π)||f||². (2) FALSE: The Fourier series converges pointwise a.e. (Carleson 1966), but not necessarily everywhere. (3) TRUE: Riemann-Lebesgue lemma. (4) TRUE: Fourier series converges in L²-norm (mean-square).

question.single: falsetrueabc Parseval's identity holds: \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}|c_n|^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}|f(x)|^2\,dx\). The Fourier series converges pointwise everywhere to \(f\). The Fourier coefficients \(c_n \to 0\) as \(|n|\to\infty\) (Riemann-Lebesgue lemma). The Fourier series of \(f\) converges to \(f\) in \(L^2[-\pi,\pi]\) norm.

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about second-order linear PDEs \(Au_{xx}+Bu_{xy}+Cu_{yy}+\ldots=0\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: B²−4AC > 0 ↔ hyperbolic (e.g., wave equation). (2) TRUE: B²−4AC = 0 ↔ parabolic (e.g., heat equation). (3) TRUE: B²−4AC < 0 ↔ elliptic (e.g., Laplace equation). (4) TRUE: Elliptic PDEs satisfy the maximum principle.

answer.text: Elliptic PDEs satisfy the maximum principle.

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about normed spaces?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(4)

(1) TRUE: Every finite-dimensional normed space is complete (Banach). (2) TRUE: ℓ² is a Hilbert space (complete inner product space). (3) FALSE: C[0,1] with sup-norm is complete (Banach), but not a Hilbert space (the parallelogram law fails for general functions). (4) TRUE: A normed space is complete iff every absolutely convergent series converges.

answer.text: Every finite-dimensional normed space over \(\mathbb{R}\) is complete.
\(C[0,1]\) with the sup-norm is a Hilbert space.
A normed space is a Banach space if and only if every absolutely convergent series converges.

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about the Poisson distribution with parameter \(\lambda > 0\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: E[X]=λ. (2) TRUE: Var[X]=λ. (3) TRUE: Poisson is the limit of Bin(n,p) as n→∞, p→0, np→λ. (4) TRUE: Sum of independent Poisson(λ₁) and Poisson(λ₂) is Poisson(λ₁+λ₂).

answer.text: Poisson\((\lambda)\) is the limiting distribution of \(\mathrm{Binomial}(n,\lambda/n)\) as \(n\to\infty\).

questiontext.text: Which of the following convergence theorems for Lebesgue integration are TRUE?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3)

(1) TRUE: Dominated Convergence Theorem (DCT). (2) TRUE: Monotone Convergence Theorem (MCT) for non-negative increasing sequences. (3) TRUE: Fatou's lemma. (4) FALSE: Uniform convergence is sufficient but not necessary for interchanging limit and integral.

answer.text: If \(|f_n|\leq g\) with \(g\) integrable, and \(f_n\to f\) a.e., then \(\int f_n\to\int f\).
If \(0\leq f_n\nearrow f\) a.e., then \(\int f_n\nearrow\int f\) (possibly \(+\infty\)).
\(\int\liminf f_n\,d\mu \leq \liminf\int f_n\,d\mu\) for non-negative measurable \(f_n\) (Fatou's lemma).
Pointwise convergence alone is sufficient to interchange \(\lim\) and \(\int\).

questiontext.text: Which of the following about matrix decompositions are TRUE?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Every matrix has a QR decomposition (by Gram-Schmidt or Householder). (2) TRUE: Schur's theorem: every square matrix is unitarily similar to an upper triangular matrix. (3) TRUE: Jordan normal form: over ℂ, every matrix is similar to a Jordan matrix. (4) TRUE: Spectral theorem for normal matrices.

answer.text: Every real matrix \(A\) with linearly independent columns has a QR decomposition \(A=QR\).
Every complex square matrix is unitarily similar to an upper triangular matrix (Schur decomposition).
Every complex square matrix is similar (over \(\mathbb{C}\)) to a Jordan normal form matrix.
Every normal matrix (\(AA^*=A^*A\)) is unitarily diagonalizable.

name.text: P01 Q55 MSQ - Residues and contour integration

questiontext.text: Which of the following about residues and contour integrals are TRUE?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(4)

(1) TRUE: Residue theorem. (2) TRUE: Residue at a simple pole z=a is lim_{z→a}(z−a)f(z). (3) FALSE: Res[f,a] at a pole of order m is the coefficient of (z−a)^{−1} in the Laurent series, which may be nonzero even for higher-order poles. (4) TRUE: ∫₋∞^∞ e^{−x²}dx = √π (by contour integration or Gaussian integral).

answer.text: \(\oint_C f(z)\,dz = 2\pi i\sum_k \mathrm{Res}[f, z_k]\) (residue theorem, sum over poles inside \(C\)).
The residue of \(f\) at a simple pole \(z=a\) is \(\lim_{z\to a}(z-a)f(z)\).
The residue at a pole of order \(m>1\) is always zero.
